questions.description: "Section 205 of the Companies Act 1963 provides the shareholders of a company with a more flexible, more available and…
questions.text: "Section 205 of the Companies Act 1963 provides the shareholders of a company with a more flexible, more available and more powerful remedy against directors' wrongdoing than any or all of the exceptions to the Rule in Foss v Harbottle". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's Case and Regulation 6 of SI 163 of 1973 are effective in…
questions.text: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's Case and Regulation 6 of SI 163 of 1973 are effective in ensuring the enforceability of a contract that has been entered into by a company, acting by its directors". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Noddy and Gilbert are the only directors and equal 50:50 shareholders in… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Noddy and Gilbert are the only directors and equal 50:50 shareholders in Small Animal Hay Limited (the "Company") which grows hay and straw and packages it for sale to pet shops as feedstuffs and bedding for rabbits, hamsters and other small animals. The Company owns approximately 2 acres of land in north County Dublin from which it harvests hay and straw. An opportunity has arisen for the Company to acquire a further 2 acres of adjoining land from another company called Home Farm Limited of which Gilbert is the sole shareholder and director with his friend Gerard. The proposed purchase price is €200,000; alternatively, Home Farm Limited is prepared to lease the land to the Company at a rent of €20,000 per annum. You are required to advise all parties on their obligations under: a) Section 194 of the Companies Act 1963, b) Sections 29 and 31 of the Companies Act 1990 and c) Directors' duties at common law. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Foreign Holidays Limited (the "Company") has operated as a travel agency… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Foreign Holidays Limited (the "Company") has operated as a travel agency organising trips, primarily to Iceland, for a number of years. Business was very badly affected by the volcanic activity there which disrupted travel not only in Iceland but also across Europe. Sean and Seamus, the Company's directors and 50:50 shareholders decided to diversify and last year invested in a new offering-cruises to the Mediterranean. Having established contracts and been on a number of fact-finding cruises, Sean and Seamus launched their new offering in October last and had a great many enquiries from people who were interested in making bookings. In addition, in the last number of weeks a significant number of bookings were made. The recent sinking of a cruise ship has, however, prompted large scale cancellations permitted under the Company's contracts with customers if done 6 months before the cruise. Sean and Seamus have reviewed the Company's books of account and see that the Company's financial position is hopeless. The Company has begun to let go all 10 employees as it could not continue to pay their wages and to make ends meet, has even dipped into the PAYE/PRSI it retained from its employees instead of remitting it to the revenue commissioners. The rent is also overdue on the Company's premises and cruise companies are now agitating for payment for bookings made by the Company which will not now be fulfilled on account of the cancellations by its customers. Sean and Seamus estimate that the company's debts exceed its assets by some €1 million. You are required to advise Sean and Seamus on the possibility that they may be the subject of an application for restriction under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 and in particular the defences that will be available to them. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard recently incorporated his carpentry business which employs eight carpenters; for many years, Richard had…
questions.text: Richard recently incorporated his carpentry business which employs eight carpenters; for many years, Richard had operated as a sole trader. The new company is called Wooden World Limited (the "Company"). One of the reasons Richard incorporated his business was because Joe, who has worked for him for 10 years, has been pressing to become his partner. Richard was not anxious to create a partnership and instead has offered him 20% of the shares in the Company for €30,000, which is acceptable to Joe. Richard has agreed with Joe that Joe's 20% of the shares will confer the right on the holder to be a director and that this would be provided for in the articles of association. Richard comes to you for advice on how to structure the sales of shares. While Richard is very happy to have Joe owning shares in the company, he is concerned that Joe might sell them to someone else and he asks you to outline the options open to him to safeguard his position. Specifically, Richard asks your advice on what might happen if Joe wished to sell his shares to someone Richard did not approve of or what would happen to the shares if Joe died. Joe also asks whether the Company's articles of association should contain something more than Model Regulation 3 of part II of Table A. You are required to provide Richard with the advice he has sought and in your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "A floating charge is a charge over a company's present or future property, or classes of property, which hovers over…
questions.text: "A floating charge is a charge over a company's present or future property, or classes of property, which hovers over that property until the moment of crystallisation whereupon the charge fastens onto the charged property or class or property, and becomes a quasi-fixed charge". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Roderick and Robert operate a roofing business and employ eight people. Company.
questions.text: Roderick and Robert operate a roofing business and employ eight people. Their work involves putting tiles and slates on the roofs of houses and industrial premises. By its nature the work is dangerous and despite being committed to safe work practices, there have been several accidents over the years. Most recently one of their employees fell from a roof in Ranelagh and broke his back, leaving him paraplegic. Roderick's and Robert's insurance covered the €1,600,000 compensation which was required to be paid to the injured employee however, in addition to a big increase in their insurance premium, Roderick and Robert were told by the insurance company that their cover was been capped at €500,000 for this year. The business is currently organised as a partnership and Roderick and Robert fear that their personal assets may be exposed were there to be another serious accident. You are required to advise Roderick and Robert on the advantages [and]{.underline} consequences of incorporating their business as a private company limited by shares. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: George is the liquidator of Second Hand Arts Limited (the "Company") which… Company, Director.
questions.text: George is the liquidator of Second Hand Arts Limited (the "Company") which has been placed into official liquidation following the bringing of a petition by the revenue commissioners grounded on the Company's inability to pay its debts. George seeks your advice on the validity of the following transactions: (a) Eighteen months before the presentation of the petition, the Company had created a floating charge over its stock in trade in favour of its sole member and managing director, John, to secure a loan of €50,000 made by John to the Company. There is evidence that the loan made by John was advanced in cash the day after the floating charge was created. (b) Two cheques were drawn and dated one month before the petition was presented to the High Court to have the Company wound up. The first cheque for €15,000 was payable to John and represented the repayment of part of the loans made 18 months earlier. This cheque was cashed the same day it was drawn. The second cheque for €30,000 was payable to a creditor of the company for goods purchased by the Company and that cheque was cashed the day after the petition was presented. You are required to advise George on the transactions described in (a) and (b) and in your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: The effect of the amendments to section 33 of the Companies Act 1963 (made by section 7 of the Investment Funds…
questions.text: The effect of the amendments to section 33 of the Companies Act 1963 (made by section 7 of the Investment Funds Companies and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 2005) has been to blur the distinction between private companies and public companies". Do you agree with this statement? In answering this question you are required to cite all relevant and statue law in support of your answer.

"Generally speaking this principle [i.e. that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members] and the statutory rules of the company law in which the principle is implicit apply to the relationship between holding companies and subsidiaries and to transactions between them and third parties". Re Frederick Inns Ltd [1991] ILRM 582 (HC) at 587 (Lardner J) You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statue law in support of your answer.

questions.description: You are required to advise the directors of Crumbs Bakery Limited (the… Regulation, Company.
questions.text: You are required to advise the directors of Crumbs Bakery Limited (the "Company"). The Company is a private company limited by shares which has 15 shareholders. Five of the shareholders hold 10 shares each and are the five brothers who incorporated the company to take over the bakery from their father who had operated as a sole trader. The other ten shareholders are employees who each hold 5 shares. The Company has six directors -- five of whom are brothers and the sixth is nominated by the employee shareholders. The directors require your advice in relation to the following two applications to have shares registered. The two applications are: (a) One of the employees, Jack died recently and his widow has lodged a stock transfer form electing to be registered as a member of the Company in relation to Jack's 5 shares. The directors want to know their rights and obligations in relation to deciding whether or not to register Jack's widow as a member. (b) The second application concerns a request to transfer the 5 shares held by one employee who is retiring (Fred) to a new employee who is joining (Jason). The directors are disposed to facilitating this transfer but have noticed that the stock transfer form has not been stamped by the Revenue Commissioners. You should note that the Company's articles of association incorporate the Regulations contained in part II of table A. You are required to advise the directors on (a) [and]{.underline} (b). [Note you must answer (a) and (b), which carry equal marks, separately.]{.underline} You must cite all relevant case and statute law in your answers.

questions.description: Shelia's Shelves Limited (the "company") is in the business of… Company, Director.
questions.text: Shelia's Shelves Limited (the "company") is in the business of manufacturing and distributing wooden shelves for use in homes. The Company is a single-member private limited company that is owned by Sheila and its directors are Sheila, her husband Woody, and Jack who is the Company's marketing manager. Jack has worked for the Company for just under four years and was employed to assist the Company increase its sales by establishing an internet presence. Jack's endeavours have been very successful and the Company's turnover in 2009 was three times what it was when Jack was appointed marketing manager. Sheila was so impressed with Jack's performance and success that she agreed to his appointment to the Board of Directors in 2010. On Jack's appointment as a director, in addition to his having been given a substantial salary increase, Sheila indicated that she would transfer Jack 10% of the Company's shares in 2012 if he continued to increase sales. In June 2012 Jack went to Sheila and asked her when he could expect to receive his shares, given that turnover was up 25%. When Sheila was not as forthcoming as Jack would have liked, Jack decided he would consider his options. For the next few weeks Jack carried out private research into setting up his own business. He has sourced a small factory in Poland where the component parts of shelves can be made for 10% of the cost of raw material and labour in Ireland and even allowing for the transportation costs, Jack is convinced that he can have his shelves on sale in Ireland for 60% of the cost of the Company's shelves. Jack has also started to take advance orders for the shelves from large retail stores that currently give orders to the Company. Last Friday Jack handed his notice to Sheila, resigning from the Company as director and employee. On telling Sheila why he was leaving and what he was going to do, Jack was shocked to be told by Sheila that he would be hearing from the Company's solicitors in connection with his flagrant breaches of duty as a director of the Company. You are required to advise Jack as to the possible action that the Company might initiate against him and on his options. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Regulation 80 of part 1 of table A is the cornerstone of corporate governance in Irish companies".
questions.text: "Regulation 80 of part 1 of table A is the cornerstone of corporate governance in Irish companies". Do you agree with this statement? In answering this question you are required to cite all relevant case and statue law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Irish Books Limited (the "Company") is an on-line book shop operating from… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Irish Books Limited (the "Company") is an on-line book shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Company has a term loan and overdraft from Big Bank plc (the "Bank") secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a first charge over its premises. Its borrowings are also secured by a separate fixed charge over its book debts. Book debts are paid into a separate account which the Bank can freeze at its discretion. Due to poor turnover, the Company stopped repaying its loans to the Bank which than appointed a receiver. Last month the directors placed the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. Following his appointment the Company's liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: (a) Through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favour of the Bank comprising the fixed charge over its premises which contained the power to appoint the receiver was [not]{.underline} registered in the Companies Registration Office under Section 99 of the Companies Act 1963; and (b) The charge over the account into which rent was paid to the Company [was]{.underline} registered under section 99 of the Companies Act 1963 in the Companies Registration Office as a fixed charge over the Company's book debts; however, the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the due to them ahead of sums due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) [which should be clearly distinguished in your answer as both parts carry equal marks]{.underline}. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Section 218 of the Companies Act 1963 is a statutory example of the Pari Passu Principle which requires all creditors…
questions.text: "Section 218 of the Companies Act 1963 is a statutory example of the Pari Passu Principle which requires all creditors of an insolvent company to be placed on an equal footing and not "leap frog" the statutory queue ordained by the Companies Acts". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statue law in your answer.

questions.description: Noddy and Gilbert are equal shareholders and directors in Blue Rinse… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Noddy and Gilbert are equal shareholders and directors in Blue Rinse Limited (the "Company") which has been in the business of selling cultural holidays to retired middle class people since 2000. Business was very strong up until 2009 and whilst the Company has a core staff of 12 employees, at times it employed up to 30 people. In the last few years, business has never been worse following the financial crisis and the plunge in bank shares and consequently the non-payment of dividends to the folk who have traditionally made up its customer base. Existing customers who previously had a number of extended weekends in Paris, Vienna and other cities are staying at home and making good with the annual Wexford Opera festival. Noddy and Gilbert have cut staff salaries by 20% and have stopped taking a salary for themselves. Even so, the Company is struggling to keep afloat and is behind on the rent for its premises and its bank loan of some €2,500,000 secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a floating charge and a fixed charge on the Company's premises which is now worth only €1,750,000. This month is it unlikely to be able to pay to the Revenue Commissioners the full amount of VAT it collected. Noddy and Gilbert believe that if the Company can only keep afloat for the next 6 months, it stands a good chance of surviving as they believe the worst of the recession is over. Noddy has approached an old business colleague, now retired, and asked him to become an investor in the company offering him 25% of the Company (12 ½ % each from him and Gilbert). Matters came to a head yesterday when the Company's bank appointed a receiver on account of the Company's bank loan being in arrears. Noddy and Gilbert approach you for advice on the following matters: (a) The steps involved in bringing a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company : (b) The likelihood of success of a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; [And]{.underline} (c) The consequence for the Company and its creditors of an examiner being appointed. You are required to advise Noddy and Gilbert in relation to the matters set out at (a), (b) [and]{.underline} (c) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. You are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law in your answer.
